framework.name: GCC Market Entry Assessment Criteria
framework.purpose: Assess whether a European FMCG company is a viable candidate for GCC market entry

dimensions.name: Market Opportunity
dimensions.description: Assesses size of addressable market, sales channels, distribution infrastructure, and European-origin preference in GCC
questions: What is the size of the addressable market in GCC (Saudi Arabia / UAE) for this specific product category? Is it growing, stable, or declining?
What is the category's import value and growth rate over the last 3 years? What is the 5-year projection?
Which channels are relevant for this product — modern trade (retail chains), foodservice, specialty / fine food, online, pharmacy/health stores, HoReCa?
Are there active GCC distribution partners who currently handle or are actively seeking products in this category? Have any named a specific product gap?
Is there a structural or consumer preference for European-origin product in this category — quality perception, origin recognition, ingredient standards, regulatory advantage?

strong.criteria: Market >$200M in category, or growing >8% annually. 2+ relevant sales channels identified. 3+ distribution partners actively seeking this type of product. Clear European-origin preference documented.

moderate.criteria: Market $50–200M or growing 3–8%. 1–2 viable channels. 1–2 potential distribution partners identified but not actively seeking. Moderate consumer preference for imports.

weak.criteria: Market <$50M, declining, or dominated by local production with no structural import demand. No identifiable distribution channel or active partners for this category.

dimensions.name: Product Readiness
dimensions.description: Assesses whether product can physically and commercially enter GCC market in reasonable timeframe (certification, shelf life, variety, labeling)
questions: Does the company hold Halal certification from a GCC-recognised body? If not, is there a clear certification path and a realistic timeline?
Is SFDA (Saudi Arabia) or ADAFSA (UAE) product registration achieved or achievable? Are there other mandatory country-specific approvals (e.g. health certificate, import permit category)?

What is the product's declared shelf life? GCC distributors typically require a minimum of 70% remaining shelf life on arrival — factoring in a 6–8 week sea freight transit. Does the product meet this threshold?
How many SKUs does the company offer in the relevant category? Is there sufficient range to build a viable distributor relationship, or is this a single-product entry with limited commercial depth?
Are labels GCC-compliant — Arabic language text, nutritional information in required format, country-of-origin declaration, net weight in metric?

strong.criteria: Halal certified from a GCC-recognised body. SFDA/ADAFSA registration achieved or clear path (<3 months). Shelf life allows 70%+ on arrival. Multi-SKU portfolio (5+ SKUs). Labels GCC-ready or minor adjustments only.

moderate.criteria: Halal certification in progress or achievable within 6 months. Regulatory path identified, 3–9 month timeline. Shelf life borderline (60–70% remaining on arrival). Limited variety (2–4 SKUs). Label adjustments required but company willing.

weak.criteria: No Halal certification and no clear path, or certification from a non-recognised body. Shelf life insufficient for import transit. Single SKU only. Labels non-compliant with no plan to adapt.

dimensions.name: Commercial Viability
dimensions.description: Assesses whether commercial structure works for company, GCC distributor, and Integrate Us (price competitiveness, margin structure, company size, MOQ flexibility)
questions: What is the estimated landed cost in GCC (product cost + freight + duty + local logistics), and how does it compare to equivalent competing products currently on shelf in the target market?
After a typical GCC distributor markup (30–40%) and Integrate Us commission, does the product still land at a competitive shelf price? Is there margin headroom for introductory pricing?
What is the company's annual revenue and production capacity? Are they large enough to reliably service a new export market without disrupting existing commitments?
Are they small enough that the GCC market represents a meaningful commercial priority — not a minor add-on ignored when domestic demand spikes?
What are the minimum order quantities, and are these realistic for an early-stage market entry where volumes start small and grow over 12–24 months?

strong.criteria: Price competitive with 40%+ margin headroom after distributor and Integrate Us layers. Mid-sized company with proven export capacity and infrastructure. MOQs flexible and realistic for market entry volumes. Dedicated export function.

moderate.criteria: Pricing borderline — margins tight but potentially workable with pricing adjustments or intro-period terms. Company has some export experience. MOQs negotiable. No dedicated export function but willing to assign one.

weak.criteria: Structurally uncompetitive on price — insufficient margin for the distribution layer. Company too small (capacity risk under export load) or too large (GCC not a strategic priority). MOQs unrealistic for entry-stage volumes.

dimensions.name: Strategic Fit & Adaptability
dimensions.description: Assesses whether company is worth investing time in building a market for (long-term orientation, PMF flexibility, track record, resource commitment)
questions: PMF flexibility — product: Is the company willing to adapt product specifications (recipe, packaging format, sizing, labelling) for the GCC market if distributors or market testing require it?
PMF flexibility — price: Can the company offer distributor-exclusive pricing, volume-based tiers, introductory pricing, or deferred payment terms to support market penetration?

Long-term vs. short-term: Does the company have a genuine long-term commitment to GCC market development (3+ year horizon), or are they seeking quick, transactional results with no appetite for market-building?
Track record: Has the company demonstrated adaptability in prior export markets — adjusting product, pricing, or commercial structure based on in-market feedback?
Resource commitment: Has the company allocated budget, personnel, or dedicated capacity to this market entry effort — or is this being run as a side project?

strong.criteria: Explicitly long-term oriented — 3+ year commitment stated and backed by resource allocation. High PMF flexibility on both product and price. Demonstrated adaptation in prior export markets. Dedicated budget confirmed.

moderate.criteria: Mixed signals — long-term interest stated but short-term pressure visible ('we need results this year'). Some flexibility on pricing, limited on product specs. Limited prior export adaptation experience. No dedicated budget confirmed.

weak.criteria: Short-term expectations — looking for results in 3–6 months. Rigid product and price position, unwilling to adapt for market requirements. No prior export adaptation. No allocated resources. This will consume Integrate Us time without returns.

dimensions.name: Decision-Maker Quality
dimensions.description: Assesses quality of company's decision-maker (authority, realism about timelines/costs, operational accessibility)
questions: Who is the decision-maker engaged with Integrate Us — founder, export director, sales manager? Do they have the authority to sign contracts, commit budgets, and approve product or pricing changes?
Are they realistic about market entry timelines? GCC typically takes 12–18 months to first meaningful revenue. Do they understand and accept this, or are they anchored on shorter expectations?
Do they understand the cost structure of GCC market entry — certification costs, distributor margin requirements, Integrate Us engagement fees, and the regulatory timeline?
Is the decision-maker operationally accessible — responsive to messages, available for regular alignment calls, able to make decisions without excessive internal approval layers?

strong.criteria: Senior decision-maker (founder or export director) with full budget authority. Realistic on timelines and cost structure — no re-education required. Actively engaged and operationally responsive. Decisions made directly, no committee approval.

moderate.criteria: Decision-maker accessible but not final authority — needs internal approval for budget or product changes. Some unrealistic timeline expectations but open to education. Responsive, but decisions take time.

weak.criteria: Junior or non-authoritative contact with no access to budget decisions. Fundamentally unrealistic on timelines or costs, and resistant to being corrected. Unresponsive or difficult to engage. This relationship will stall before it delivers.

go.next_steps: Prepare and issue engagement proposal immediately. Assign account lead. Begin onboarding within 2 weeks. Agree retainer level and commission structure based on product category and company size.

conditional.next_steps: Identify which dimensions fell short and set named conditions with deadlines. Use the gaps as a structured onboarding conversation with the company. Reassess within 30–60 days. Do not issue a full proposal until conditions are addressed.

revisit.next_steps: Company has potential but is not ready. Document specific gaps clearly. Provide honest feedback if appropriate. Set a 6-month calendar reassessment. Do not invest further time until fundamental gaps — product certification, PMF flexibility, or decision-maker — are resolved.

no_go.next_steps: Document the rationale in full. Archive the assessment. Do not allocate further resources. If the company was an inbound inquiry, respond professionally — market conditions may change and a future reassessment may be warranted.
